title: Definable and Contractible Contracts
abstract: This paper analyzes Bayesian normal form games in which players write contracts that condition their actions on the contracts of other players. These contracts are required to be representable in a formal language. This is accomplished by constructing contracts which are definable functions of the Godel code of every other player's contract. We provide a complete characterization of the set of allocations supportable as pure-strategy Bayesian equilibria of this contracting game. When information is complete, this characterization provides a folk theorem. In general, the set of supportable allocations is smaller than the set supportable by a centralized mechanism designer.
summary_en: In many real strategic settings, players write contracts that condition their own behaviour on what other players are doing — essentially, contracts about contracts. This paper makes that idea mathematically precise: it requires contracts to be writable in a formal language, using Gödel codes of other players' contracts. The authors fully characterise which allocations can be supported as pure-strategy Bayesian equilibria of this "contracting game." Under complete information, they establish a folk theorem. In general, fewer allocations are supportable than under a centralised mechanism designer. A foundational technical contribution to mechanism design.
policy_relevance: Primarily academic. The decentralised contracting framework underlies analysis of smart-contract networks, blockchain coordination, and self-enforcing agreements — increasingly policy-relevant areas. Caveat: the result is abstract; implementation in specific Hungarian regulatory contexts (e.g., Web3 policy) requires domain-specific mapping.
